coredata.dc:title: Engineering chiral carbon dots for triple-mode discrimination of tryptophan enantiomers
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Chang, Dan
Wang, Shuo
Yan, Yanan
Feng, Jianyang
Pei, Jianan
Shi, Hu
Yang, Yongxing
Shi, Lihong
Shuang, Shaomin
Dong, Chuan
coredata.dc:description: In situ chiral identification of amino acid enantiomers remains challenging because most emerging alternative techniques still rely on professional instruments and skilled operators. Moreover, single-mode monitoring is prone to experimental errors due to instrument variations and nonstandard analytical procedures. Herein, we propose a triple read-out sensing platform for chiral discrimination of tryptophan (Trp) enantiomers. Chiral carbon dots (L/D-CDs) are synthesized via a one-pot hydrothermal route using p-phenylenediamine and L/D-tartaric acid without chiral modification. They exhibit dual-emission behaviors at 420 nm and 614 nm under 365 nm excitation. Under optimal conditions, L-CDs effectively differentiate Trp enantiomers, leading to simultaneous variations in circular dichroism and ratiometric fluorescence and establishing a foundation for multi-mode L-Trp detection. When integrated with a smartphone equipped with a custom-programmed applet, a portable handheld sensor integrated L-CDs, 96-well microplates, and a 3D-printed device is constructed for in situ L-Trp quantification. The instrument-free, direct read-out chiral sensor demonstrates good stability, acceptable reproducibility, and high selectivity, enabling intelligent L-Trp detection in real samples. This work may open new avenues for the rational design of multi-mode chiral sensors for in situ enantioselective recognition.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Triple-mode detection
Chiral recognition
Tryptophan enantiomers
Handheld sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Circular dichroism and ratiometric FL dual-mode detection of L-Trp
Construction of handheld chiral sensor for L-Trp identification
Design of the applet for imaging and direct reading of target concentration
Circular dichroic and ratiometric FL dual-mode sensing of L-Trp
Selectivity and anti-interference ability of L-CDs
Detection mechanism of L-CDs to L-Trp
In situ determination of L-Trp by instrument-free handheld ratiometric FL sensor
Circular dichroism, ratiometric FL, and handheld sensing of L-Trp in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Synthetic strategy of L/D-CDs. (B − E) TEM and size distribution of L/D-CDs, inset: HRTEM image. (F − G) AFM image of L/D-CDs, inset: height distribution. (H) XRD pattern of L/D-CDs. (I) Raman spectra of L/D-CDs. (J) FT-IR spectra of L/D-CDs.
(A) XPS spectra of L/D-CDs. (B − D) High-resolution C1s, N1s, and O1s peaks of L/D-CDs.

(A–B) UV–vis absorption and FL emission spectra of L/D-CDs. (C–D) Emission spectra of L/D-CDs. (E) Tauc plot of L/D-CDs. (F) XPS valence-band spectra of L/D-CDs. (G) Schematic diagram of possible FL mechanism of L/D-CDs.

(A) CD spectra of L/D-TA. (B) CD spectra of L/D-CDs at 160°C. (C) CD spectra of L/D-CDs at different temperatures. (D) CD spectra of L/D-CDs at different reaction times. (E–F) FL spectra of L/D-CDs at different reaction times.

(A) CD spectra of L-CDs with different L-Trp concentration. (B) Relationship between CD (mdeg) and different L-Trp concentration. (C) CD spectra of L-CDs for Trp enantiomers. (D) FL spectra of L-CDs after treatment with L-Trp. (E) Curve of the I420/I614 versus L-Trp concentration, inset: linear relationship between Ln(I420/I614) and L-Lrp concentration. (F) FL spectra of L-CDs for Trp enantiomers.
Specificity research results to different interfering substances.

(A) TEM image and size distribution of L-CDs@L-Trp. (B) AFM image and height distribution of L-CDs@L-Trp. (C) Decay curves of L-CDs after incubation with L-Trp. (D) Zeta potentials of L-CDs and L-CDs@L-Trp. (E) Absorption spectra of L-CDs in the presence and absence of L-Trp. (F) FT-IR spectra of L-CDs and L-CDs@L-Trp.
Schematic diagram of molecular docking between L-CDs and L/D-Trp (Hydrogen bonding and hydrophobic interactions are depicted using green and purple dashed lines, respectively).
(A) Schematic diagram of the handheld sensor. (B) Operation demonstration for in situ monitoring.

(A) Image acquisition by the integrated handheld box and smartphone. (B) Heat map derived from the R/B value for different L-Trp concentration. (C) Corresponding color change (R/B) versus the concentration of L-Trp. (D) PCA score plots of L-CDs-based sensor against various concentration of L-Trp. (E) Linear relationship between PC 1 and L-Trp concentration. (F) Heat map derived from the R/B value for various analytes.
Flowcharts for image processing of a smartphone with built-in applet.
Schematic illustration of L-CDs design and applications of triple-modal sensing of Trp enantiomers.
Binding free energies for L-Trp and D-Trp docking on L-CDs.

head.ce:title: Engineering chiral carbon dots for triple-mode discrimination of tryptophan enantiomers

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Environmental Science and College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Environmental Science and College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Environmental Science and College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In situ chiral identification of amino acid enantiomers remains challenging because most emerging alternative techniques still rely on professional instruments and skilled operators. Moreover, single-mode monitoring is prone to experimental errors due to instrument variations and nonstandard analytical procedures. Herein, we propose a triple read-out sensing platform for chiral discrimination of tryptophan (Trp) enantiomers. Chiral carbon dots (L/D-CDs) are synthesized via a one-pot hydrothermal route using p-phenylenediamine and L/D-tartaric acid without chiral modification. They exhibit dual-emission behaviors at 420 nm and 614 nm under 365 nm excitation. Under optimal conditions, L-CDs effectively differentiate Trp enantiomers, leading to simultaneous variations in circular dichroism and ratiometric fluorescence and establishing a foundation for multi-mode L-Trp detection. When integrated with a smartphone equipped with a custom-programmed applet, a portable handheld sensor integrated L-CDs, 96-well microplates, and a 3D-printed device is constructed for in situ L-Trp quantification. The instrument-free, direct read-out chiral sensor demonstrates good stability, acceptable reproducibility, and high selectivity, enabling intelligent L-Trp detection in real samples. This work may open new avenues for the rational design of multi-mode chiral sensors for in situ enantioselective recognition.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Chirality is ubiquitous in nature and is a fundamental intrinsic property of numerous life-sustaining molecules, such as amino acids, nucleic acids, and proteins [1,2]. The human body exhibits remarkable chiral selectivity, responding differently to different enantiomers [3]. Enantiomers are pairs of non-superimposable mirror images of each other, analogous to left and right hands, which are structurally identical yet non-overlapping. Despite their similar physical and chemical properties, enantiomers can exhibit significantly different pharmacological and biomedical activities due to their interactions with chiral receptors in the body [4–6]. Consequently, chiral discrimination of enantiomers is of significant importance in pharmaceutics, medicine, and life sciences.

As the molecular building blocks of life, amino acids (AAs) are chiral molecules (D- and L-forms), except for glycine [7]. While enantiomers of AAs exhibit identical physicochemical properties in non-biological environments, they play distinct or even opposing roles in living systems [8,9]. For instance, L-tryptophan (L-Trp) is an essential amino acid involved in protein synthesis and serves as a biosynthetic precursor to critical neurologically active substances such as serotonin and melatonin. Imbalance or deficiency of L-Trp is closely linked to various chronic diseases, including diabetes, depression, and hepatic disorders [10]. In contrast, D-Trp, a nonproteinogenic AA, does not participate in human metabolism but is a key intermediate in synthesizing polypeptide antibiotics and immunosuppressive agents [7]. In this regard, developing rapid, intelligent, and convenient chiral sensors for enantioselective analysis is highly desirable.

Diversified methodologies for enantioselective recognition, including nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy, surface-enhanced Raman scattering and gas chromatography have been exploited [11–15]. Although these methods possess high accuracy and good repeatability, they necessitate lengthy processing times, equipped laboratory facilities, and highly skilled personnel, making accurate self-monitoring and timely point-of-care testing difficult. Encouragingly, enantioselective fluorescence (FL) technology has garnered significant attention due to its advantages in visual detection, simple operation, low cost, and high selectivity and sensitivity [16]. Moreover, compared to conventional analytical instruments, FL-based testing systems can be integrated into portable handheld devices, enabling rapid in situ detection in food safety, environmental monitoring, and water quality assessment. Up to date, emerging chiral FL materials have been developed for chiral recognition and detection, including polymers (1, 1′-Bi-2-napthol BINOL), molecularly imprinted polymers, and metal–organic frameworks [17–22]. However, current chiral FL nanomaterial-based sensors face several limitations, including reliance on expensive reagents and complex synthetic procedures (Table S1). Given the issues raised above, developing green and efficient methods for enantiomer identification remains a significant and unfulfilled challenge.

Carbon dots (CDs), a highly promising class of carbon-based zero-dimensional materials, have recently attracted considerable attention. Due to their easy preparation, unique optical properties, and excellent biocompatibility, CDs exhibit remarkable advantages for various applications, ranging from sensing to bio/photoelectrocatalysis [23]. As an emerging subclass of CDs, chiral CDs (CCDs) are closely related to material chemistry and life sciences. By virtue of their superior steric configurations and biological activity, CCDs hold great potential in chiral catalysis, asymmetric synthesis, enantiomer discrimination, and related fields. Despite significant advances in achiral FL probes, research on chiral FL probes remains relatively limited. Notably, to our knowledge, no studies have yet focused on developing portable chiral sensors based on CDs. In light of the above facts, it is of high demand to establish novel CCDs-based sensors with user-friendly designs and in situ monitoring capabilities for widespread applications.

In this study, we develop a simple and reliable triple-mode assay platform for discriminating and identifying Trp enantiomers using label-free chiral L-CDs. As illustrated in Scheme 1 , p-phenylenediamine (PPD) and L-tartaric acid (L-TA) are ingeniously designed as precursors to prepare chiral L-CDs via a one-step hydrothermal strategy without chiral modification. This process imparts L-CDs with dual-emission FL and chiral properties. Notably, L-Trp can enhance the ratiometric FL signal of L-CDs, whereas D-Trp induces no observable change, resulting in ratiometric response signals and multicolor variations. By integrating a smartphone with a self-programming applet, the L-CDs-based handheld platform enables in situ chiral recognition of Trp enantiomers. The instrument-free, direct read-out handheld sensor facilitates rapid sample-to-answer testing and exhibits robust environmental adaptability. Compared to existing chiral sensors, our proposed sensing strategy offers triple-mode result verification and flexible detection options, which can satisfy different testing requirements based on sensitivity and available instruments, thereby improving detection accuracy and reliability. Finally, the triple-mode assay is used for determination of L-Trp in real samples with acceptable results, indicating its potential for practical applications in food safety and environmental monitoring. We establish a new framework for multi-mode enantioselective assay platforms, highlighting its advantages and promising prospects for real-world applications.
Details of characterization methods and reagents are described in Supporting Information.

Chiral CDs were prepared by a one-step hydrothermal method. The detailed synthesis processes were as follows: First, 0.02 g of PPD and 0.05 g of L-TA were dissolved in 20 mL ultrapure water, which was transferred to a Teflon-sealed autoclave and heated at 160°C for 2 h. After cooling to room temperature, the reaction solution was centrifuged for 20 min (6000 rpm) and filtered using a 0.22 μm filter membrane. Furthermore, the solid powder was obtained by freeze-drying and stored in a refrigerator at 4°C for characterization and subsequent experiments. D-CDs were synthesized by the same procedure using PPD together with D-TA as precursors. Meanwhile, CDs were prepared by the same procedure using PPD as the sole precursor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Circular dichroism and ratiometric FL dual-mode detection of L-Trp
ce:section.ce:para: In brief, 50 μL of L-CDs stock solution and 500 μL of PBS buffer solution were introduced into spectrofluorimetric cell, followed by different concentrations of L-Trp solution. After 30 s, FL spectra were recorded under 365 nm excitation and circular dichroism (CD) spectra were also measured for each mixture. The same approach was conducted to evaluate the selectivity of L-CDs to various analytes, and their FL spectra were obtained under the same conditions for comparison.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of handheld chiral sensor for L-Trp identification
ce:section.ce:para: The instrument-free, direct read-out handheld chiral FL sensor consists of an enclosed 3D dark cavity, a smartphone with a self-designed applet, 96-well microplates, and a sample pretreatment device. The whole device is printed by a commercial 3D printer using photosensitive resin as the printing materials, and sprayed with black matte coating to reduce specular reflection. In this device, the UV lamps are fixed on the four corners of the top of the box, attaching a cardan joint to adjust the position of the light source. The 96-well microplates are placed vertically below the smartphone placement window, and a fresnel lens is fixed on the top to improve the imaging quality of the microplate by calibrating the light source. The sample pretreatment system is equipped in the 3D holder, which is used for removing large suspended solids and insoluble impurities to minimize the interference as far as possible. For in situ measurement, different concentrations of L-Trp solution containing L-CDs were dropped into the 96-well microplates. After the complete reaction, the 96-well microplates were inserted into the handheld device. Through the photographic hole, the full image of 96-well microplates containing sample solutions can be acquired with a smartphone for later quantitative detection. A series of FL images were analyzed by a custom-built applet. Afterward, the concentration values are read out directly and displayed on the smartphone screen.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design of the applet for imaging and direct reading of target concentration
ce:section.ce:para: To provide the end-user a stand-alone handheld sensor, we design an intelligent applet (namely, “point-of-care testing”) for imaging the 96-well microplates and in situ reading of L-Trp concentration. The most important function of the applet is to input the FL images, select the calibration curves, and display the determined L-Trp on the smartphone screen.
The application in actual samples is displayed in Supporting Information.

Suitable precursors are crucial to the properties of L/D-CDs. To obtain ideal chiral L/D-CDs featuring long-wavelength emission, several key factors must be considered. First, L/D-CDs should possess chiral functional groups to establish an efficient chiral system, thereby endowing them with excellent enantioselective capacity toward chiral substances. Additionally, the incorporation of heteroatoms can regulate the emission wavelength by tailoring the structural and electronic properties of CDs [24]. Moreover, a suitable aromatic structure can inherently reduce the energy gap by expanding large π-conjugated domains, which is beneficial to the formation of the conjugated FL center [25]. As displayed in Fig. 1 A, red/blue dual-emission L/D-CDs are prepared using PPD and L/D-TA via a one-pot hydrothermal method. As far as we know, PPD, a kind of cheap aromatic compound, is an ideal candidate to synthesize long-emissive FL CDs. The role of PPD is twofold. On the one hand, the built-in aromatic structure of PPD favors the formation of rigid graphene sheets with extended π conjugation. On the other hand, it contains two amino groups, which not only introduces new energy levels but also provides hydrophilic peripheral functional groups. L-TA serves as both a carbon source and a chiral stimulus, contributing to the formation of chiral structures and participating in the generation of carbon nuclei. At high temperatures, these precursors are in situ carbonized together with dehydration, polymerization, and aromatization reactions, yielding the conjugated sp2 domains with abundant surface groups [26]. Compared to the post-functionalization methods based on chemical interactions, a one-step chiral synthetic strategy using suitable precursors avoids the time-spatial separation process and greatly saves time and energy.

The morphology and size of L/D-CDs are observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM). TEM images show that L/D-CDs are spherical and uniformly distributed with the average sizes of 2.6 nm and 4.2 nm, respectively (Fig. 1B − E). The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images of L/D-CDs reveal clear lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) crystal planes of graphene [27]. AFM images (Fig. 1F − G) show that two chiral CDs are monodisperse and the average heights of L-CDs and D-CDs are 4.3 nm and 6.0 nm, indicating that two chiral CDs consist of multilayer graphene-like sheets [28]. TEM and AFM results confirm that two chiral CDs possess different carbon nuclei. The zeta potentials of L-CDs and D-CDs are −18.5 mV and −16.4 mV, which implies the presence of abundant negatively charged groups on their surfaces (Fig. S1). The crystalline state of L/D-CDs is studied by X-ray diffraction (XRD) and Raman spectra. Fig. 1H displays that both chiral CDs show typical graphitic carbon diffraction peaks at around 25°, validating the crystalline structure of L/D-CDs [29]. As shown in Fig. 1I, two Raman peaks at 1381 cm−1 and 1586 cm−1 are observed, which are ascribed to the disordered structure (D band) and graphite status (G band) of carbon materials, respectively. The results indicate that two chiral CDs have sectional disordered graphitelike structures [30].

The surface structure and elemental composition of L/D-CDs are further explored by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy (Fig. 1J). The peaks located at 1136.39 cm−1, 1309.56 cm−1, 1408.20 cm−1, 1709 cm−1, 3024.75 cm−1, and 3401.21 cm−1 demonstrate the presence of C–O, C–N, C=C, C=O, C–H, and O–H/N–H bonds, respectively. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) is also employed to study the chemical structure of L/D-CDs. The observation from Fig. 2 A indicates that two chiral CDs contain carbon (285.5 eV), nitrogen (400.9 eV), and oxygen (532.2 eV). Notably, the oxygen content in L-CDs is higher than that of D-CDs, while the nitrogen content is almost the same. The C1s band (Fig. 2B) can be divided into three components, corresponding to C=C/C–C (284.4 eV), C–O/C–N (285.8 eV), and C=O (287.9 eV). The N1s spectrum displays three peaks at 398.7 eV, 400.4 eV, and 401.1 eV for pyridinic N, amino N, and graphitic N, illustrating that the nitrogen elements are successfully doped into L/D-CDs (Fig. 2C). The O1s spectrum (Fig. 2D) has three fitting peaks that can be assigned to O–H (531.3 eV), C–O/N–O (532.2 eV), and C=O (533.0 eV). Based on the above results, it is found that two chiral CDs exhibit distinct differences in both internal structure and surface functional groups, which may be responsible for the different FL and CD properties of L/D-CDs [16,31].

The optical properties of L/D-CDs are investigated using UV–vis absorption and FL spectra. As can be seen from Fig. 3 A–B, two chiral CDs exhibit similar absorption peaks centered at 241 nm and 302 nm. The peak at 241 nm is attributed to the π-π* transition of the aromatic sp2 domains, and the peak at 302 nm is assigned to the n-π* transition of C=O or C–N [31]. Under a single excitation wavelength of 365 nm (Fig. 3C–D), both chiral CDs show two evident emission peaks at 420 nm and 614 nm. The blue emission may originate from the carbon core and the red emission may arise from the surface state [32,33]. During the hydrothermal synthesis of L/D-CDs, dehydration and condensation of the precursors result in C–N cross-linking, which gradually forms the carbon core at high temperature. The oxygen- or nitrogen-containing functional groups of the residual precursors are attached to the core surface and establish distinct surface states. The presence of surface states narrows the energy levels, resulting in red FL emission [34]. In other words, the dual-emission properties of L/D-CDs are attributed to the synergistic effect of the carbon core and surface states.

To elucidate the possible photoluminescence mechanism, the energy band gaps (Eg) of L/D-CDs are calculated using the Tauc plots. The Eg values of L-CDs and D-CDs are 3.71 eV and 3.59 eV (Fig. 3E), respectively. The valence band positions (EVB) of L/D-CDs are analyzed by XPS valence-band spectra. As shown in Fig. 3F, the EVB values of L-CDs and D-CDs are 2.19 eV and 2.32 eV, respectively. Based on the formula of Eg = EVB–ECB, the conduction band values (ECB) of L-CDs and D-CDs are calculated to be –1.52 eV and –1.27 eV, respectively. According to previous reports, a possible photoluminescence mechanism of L/D-CDs is proposed. As shown in Fig. 3G, owing to the introduction of oxygen- or nitrogen-containing functional groups, there are multiple emissive traps and different emission centers between the conduction and valence bands, resulting in the excitation-dependent FL behavior of L/D-CDs [35]. The average FL lifetimes of L-CDs and D-CDs are 2.35 ns and 2.17 ns, respectively (Fig. S2).

Furthermore, we evaluate the photostability of L/D-CDs under different conditions. As revealed in Fig. S3A–B, the FL intensities remain unchanged even after 30 min of UV irradiation, proving the eminent photostability of two chiral CDs. Except for the extreme acidic and alkaline environments, no significant difference is observed in the FL intensities of L/D-CDs in the pH range of 2.0–12.0 (Fig. S3C–D), which verifies that two chiral CDs are stable under physiological environments. Additionally, after storage for 15 days at 4°C, the FL intensities of two chiral CDs remain nearly constant (Fig. S3E–F), indicating the outstanding long-term stability of two chiral CDs. To sum up, L-CDs and D-CDs have excellent stability, which lays a solid foundation for further applications.

To illustrate the chiral origin of L/D-CDs, circular dichroism (CD) spectra are employed to confirm the chiral features of chiral precursors and their products. As depicted in Fig. 4 A, the chiral precursors exhibit corresponding mirror-image CD signatures. Interestingly, L-CDs and D-CDs show nearly symmetrical spectra with negative and positive cotton effects (Fig. 4B), but CDs prepared using PPD as the sole precursor display no CD signals (Fig. S4). Comparison of the CD signals of the chiral precursors reveals that their inherent chiral groups are retained in L/D-CDs. Next, we monitor the CD spectra of L/D-CDs under different conditions. As shown in Fig. 4C, the CD signal of L/D-CDs gradually diminish as the reaction temperature increases. This phenomenon is attributed to an increased degree of carbonization, which disrupts the chiral structure of L/D-TA and incorporates it into the carbon core [36]. To ensure the strong FL signals of L/D-CDs while preserving the chiral signals of the precursors, 160°C is chosen as the optimal reaction temperature. Moreover, we study the optical and chiral properties of L/D-CDs under different reaction times. As shown in Fig. 4D, the CD signals of L/D-CDs gradually weaken with prolonged hydrothermal reaction time, but their FL intensities show an opposite trend (Fig. 4E–F). These results suggest that longer reaction time favors the formation of fluorophore-rich structures at the expense of chiral active groups. Based on previous reports, we speculate that some chiral groups of the chiral residues are attached to the core surface of L/D-CDs, thereby giving them chiral characteristics. Specifically, L/D-CDs consist of an achiral carbon core and chiral groups attached to the core surface [37–39].

The FL response of L/D-CDs to L-Trp is shown in Fig. S5. When L-Trp is added to L/D-CDs alone, the FL intensity of L-CDs changes significantly compared with that of D-CDs. From the perspective of sensing applications, we chose L-CDs as the optimal ratiometric FL probes for the detection of L-Trp. To obtain better analytical performance, several conditions are explored and optimized using the FL intensity ratio (I420/I614) as the evaluation index. As depicted in Fig. S6A, the ratio value increases significantly with the increase of incubation time, and stabilizes when the incubation time exceeds 60 s. As a result, 60 s is considered to be the optimized incubation time for subsequent experiments. Considering the complex conditions in practical applications, we also compare the ratiometric FL detection performance of L-CDs for L-Trp under different pH. As shown in Fig. S6B, the ratio value shows an irregular trend as the pH value increases from 2.0 to 12.0. To further expand its application in point-of-care testing, pH 7.0 is selected as the optimal pH for L-Trp detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Circular dichroic and ratiometric FL dual-mode sensing of L-Trp
ce:section.ce:para: Following optimal conditions, L-CDs are used to construct a dual-readout assay platform for L-Trp detection. As presented in Fig. 5 A, the CD signal of L-CDs gradually increases with the increase of L-Trp concentration, demonstrating that L-CDs-based chiral sensing system can be applied for quantitative detection of L-Trp. The CD signal at 230 nm shows a favorable linear relationship with L-Trp concentration from 0.1 mM to 4 mM (Fig. 5B). The linear correlation can be fitted as y = 0.0054x – 1.1132 (R2 = 0.998), and the detection limit (LOD) for L-Trp is calculated to be 2.6 µM based on the triple signal-to-noise ratio. Clearly, the CD signals of L-CDs@L-Trp and L-CDs@D-Trp show opposite cotton effects, implying that L-CDs can effectively detect Trp enantiomers (Fig. 5C). Moreover, the FL changes of L-CDs after the addition of L-Trp are also recorded in Fig. 5D. Interestingly, the FL intensity at 614 nm gradually quenches, while the FL intensity at 420 nm greatly enhances with the rise of L-Trp concentration. Fig. 5E shows that the FL intensity ratio (I420/I614) of L-CDs is proportional to the concentration of L-Trp in the range of 0.1 − 4 mM. The working curve is plotted by employing Ln(I420/I614) and L-Trp concentration with a linear regression equation of y = 0.1756x + 1.9242 (R2 = 0.999). The LOD for L-Trp is determined as 0.24 µM based on the triple signal-to-noise ratio. When D-Trp is added to L-CDs, the change in FL intensity is negligible (Fig. 5F). This indicates that L-CDs can detect Trp enantiomers by ratiometric FL and circular dichroism dual modes.

Dual-emission ratiometric FL of the chiral sensing system enables sensitive dose differentiation by visualization. Surprisingly, each dosage of L-Trp matches with a distinguishable color from orange, salmon pink, lilac to sky blue under 365 nm UV lamp (inset of Fig. 5D), which provides an opportunity for visual assay of L-Trp with the naked eye. To intuitively present the results, principal component analysis (PCA) score plot constructed from RGB variations shows clear discrimination of each dosage. PCA is a classic dimensionality reduction and classification method, which is widely used for visual monitoring of analytes. Notably, six distinct L-Trp concentrations are precisely distinguished without overlap (Fig. S7A). Since the second discriminant factor (PC 2) in the PCA plots is significantly below 40 %, it is acceptable to use the first discriminant factor (PC 1) to quantify L-Trp concentration. The calibration curve shows a good linear relationship (R2 = 0.996) between PC 1 and L-Trp concentration (Fig. S7B), which further reflects the recognition capability of L-CDs-based FL sensor. In the heat map, the FL color changes induced by L-Trp are expressed as color intensity, which intuitively illustrates their inherent differences in analytes responses (Fig. S7C). This suggests that the constructed ratiometric FL and circular dichroism dual-mode sensor has the advantages of fast visual response, good sensitivity, and high sensitivity, and the dual-readout modes can complement each other.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and anti-interference ability of L-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the selectivity of L-CDs in complex sensing circumstances, the FL responses of L-CDs towards various potentially interfering substances covering common anions, cations, left- and right-handed amino acids are tested. As illustrated in Fig. 6 , no interfering substances other than the target analyte cause a significant increase in the FL intensity ratio (I420/I614) of L-CDs, which indicates that L-CDs are highly selective to L-Trp. It is noteworthy that the addition of L-Trp induces a marked enhancement in the FL intensity ratio of L-CDs, whereas no significant response is observed for D-Trp, verifying that L-CDs can differentiate between L-Trp and D-Trp, which are attributed to the important role of stereochemical selectivity in the chiral recognition [4]. Besides, anti-interference experiments are conducted in complex coexisting analogs environments. When L-Trp is added to the L-CDs solution containing various interfering analytes, the FL intensity ratio of L-CDs remains stable compared to the initial ratio, proving that L-CDs-based FL probes have robust anti-interference capabilities.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection mechanism of L-CDs to L-Trp
ce:section.ce:para: Encouraged by the above results, we discuss the possible interaction mechanism of L-CDs towards L-Trp by TEM, AFM, FL decay, zeta potential, UV–vis spectra, and FT-IR. TEM results (Fig. 7 A) show a slight aggregation of L-CDs upon reaction with L-Trp, which conforms that the size of the composite is larger than that of bare L-CDs. The representative AFM image (Fig. 7B) exhibits that the average height of L-CDs@L-Trp conjugates is 13.2 nm, which is in good agreement with TEM analysis. Obviously, the average FL lifetime of L-CDs exhibits negligible variation before and after the addition of L-Trp (Fig. 7C). The small difference between the two values indicates that the reaction mode between L-CDs and L-Trp may be related to a static process [40]. Fig. 7D reveals that L-Trp leads to the zeta potential increase from –18.5 mV to –0.2 mV, which is attributed to the formation of the stable complexes between L-CDs and L-Trp that shields the negative charges of the functional groups on the surface of L-CDs. Moreover, a new absorption band appears at 288 nm upon introducing L-Trp (Fig. 7E), reflecting the occurrence of L-CDs@L-Trp complexes. To further verify this hypothesis, we investigate the FT-IR spectra of L-CDs in the presence of L-Trp (Fig. 7F). The C=O vibrations of L-CDs@L-Trp show a significant redshift compared to L-CDs [41]. Based on these findings, we infer that the sensing mechanism is due to the formation of intermolecular hydrogen bonds between the carboxyl groups on the surface of L-CDs and L-Trp.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In situ determination of L-Trp by instrument-free handheld ratiometric FL sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Although circular dichroism/ratiometric FL dual-mode analytical methods demonstrate excellent selectivity and sensitivity, these systems lack full integration, necessitating complex pretreatment procedures, expensive laboratory equipment, and specialized manual operations. In contrast, smartphone-integrated handheld sensors show superior advantages for on-site rapid testing, particularly in resource-limited settings where affordable and user-friendly monitoring devices are urgently needed. To realize sample-to-answer integration and automated testing, we develop a fully integrated smartphone-assisted visual quantitative handheld platform utilizing 3D-printing technology for in situ monitoring of Trp enantiomers. This handheld sensor exhibits significant advantages in terms of user-friendliness and cost-effectiveness. The operational procedure for end-users involves pipetting samples into 96-well plates, inserting the plates into the handheld device, capturing images via the applet, and then obtaining the test results. For a batch of 11 samples, the entire process, including sample loading (1 − 3 min), reaction time (1 min), and data processing (2 − 4 min), can be completed within 10 min. This approach circumvents the need for complex sample pretreatment and expensive instrumentation other than a smartphone, while demonstrating high cost-effectiveness, with an estimated assay cost of $2.5. Compared to conventional laboratory-based analytical techniques, this portable and user-friendly handheld sensor significantly reduces detection costs and time.

The schematic diagram of the handheld FL sensor is shown in Fig. 9 A. The cassette-shaped device comprises four essential modules: an enclosed 3D dark cavity, a smartphone with an intelligent analysis applet, a moveable 96-well microplate, and a sample pretreatment system. The 3D-printed holder provides a stable analytical environment that ensures precise positioning of the sensing system and prevents external light interference. The top portion is equipped with a smartphone placement area and features a circular observation window with the diameter of 4 cm for photographing the 96-well plates. The smartphone, as a versatile, portable, and easy-to-use tool, undertakes image acquisition, data processing, and concentration output, greatly eliminating misjudgment by the naked eye and realizing high-precision detection. The chamber is illuminated by four miniature UV lamps (365 nm), which are mounted on the four corners at the top of the chamber. The position of the light source can be adjusted in real time to maintain a certain angle with the smartphone, minimizing errors due to changes in shooting position. To enable high-throughput on-site detection, a sample pretreatment system combining a syringe and a filter membrane is used for sample extraction and purification. When the light source, smartphone, and 96-well microplate are all assembled, the handheld sensor enables real-time FL imaging and in situ concentration analysis.

The handheld ratiometric FL sensing procedure is depicted in Fig. 9B. First, the L-CDs and target L-Trp solutions with certain concentrations are added to the 96-well plates using the accompanying syringe and allowed to stand. The plates are then placed into the handheld dark box, and a series of FL images are taken using the integrated handheld box and smartphone. Subsequently, the analysis results are displayed on the smartphone screen. As depicted in Fig. 10 A, it is evident that the gradual color transition from red to blue can be discerned as the L-Trp concentration increases. The corresponding heat map (Fig. 10B) further indicates that the instrument-free handheld FL sensor has good responsiveness to L-Trp. Fig. 10C reveals two linear relationships between the R/B value and L-Trp concentration in the ranges of 0.2–1.2 mM and 1.4–2.6 mM. Based on the triple signal-to-noise ratio, the LOD values are calculated to be 0.30 μM and 0.64 μM, respectively. Fig. 10D presents PCA distribution of L-Trp at various concentrations. Intuitively, different concentrations of L-Trp can be well differentiated in the PCA standard scoring plot, and PC 1 is proportional to L-Trp concentration in the range of 0.1 − 4 mM (Fig. 10E), indicating that the L-CDs-based handheld sensor has excellent discriminative capability for L-Trp over a broad concentration range. As shown in Fig. S9, only L-Trp causes a sharp color change from red to blue, while other representative amino acids exhibit negligible effects under identical conditions, as visualized in the heat map (Fig. 10F). These results demonstrate that the instrument-free L-CDs-based handheld FL sensor has high selectivity and robust anti-interference performance.

To further validate the robustness of the handheld sensor, we study its detection performance under complex environmental conditions. As shown in Fig. S10, the R/B values exhibit negligible variations under different temperatures and humidity levels. These results demonstrate that the proposed handheld sensor exhibits exceptional robustness under harsh conditions. Furthermore, we evaluate the effect of different smartphones on colorimetric visualization outcomes. As shown in Fig. S11, the colors of the samples captured by different smartphones are not exactly the same, but the R/B values show a synchronous decreasing trend. Consequently, different models of smartphones can be employed for L-Trp quantification by correlating the R/B value with L-Trp concentration to establish individual calibration curves, which effectively mitigates interference during image acquisition and improves the accuracy of quantitative detection. Taken together, the handheld sensor can be used as a reliable and practicable platform for on-site quantitation of L-Trp.

To enable ratiometric FL measurement portability and provide the end-user a stand-alone device, our group design an intelligent applet with minimal user intervention that allows for automated image processing, generation of calibration curves, and sharing of test results. The pseudocode for image processing and concentration calculation in WeChat program is presented in Fig. S12. The end-user operation is to open the self-compiled applet to input pictures, definite the standard function, and read the L-Trp concentration in situ. As shown in Fig. 11 , there are two acquisition strategies for FL images, namely local uploading and real-time shooting. In local uploading mode, FL images are first captured and then analyzed for concentration using the applet. The real-time shooting mode facilitates immediate data processing and in situ results display. This study adopts the local uploading model. In the “Standard curves analysis” option, the linear relationship between R/B value and L-Trp concentration is established, and the corresponding parameters (R/B value and mM concentration) are input into the “Parameter definition” fields. Finally, the tested results are obtained according to the first step of the fitted equation. In this way, visible FL changes are quickly converted into test results by the intelligent terminal platform, thereby realizing rapid in situ analysis with low usage and high throughput. This is the first instrument-free ratiometric FL chiral handheld sensor for enantioselective sensing of Trp.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Circular dichroism, ratiometric FL, and handheld sensing of L-Trp in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the applicability and reliability of the multi-mode sensing assay, the developed L-CDs are applied to determine L-Trp in real samples using the standard addition method. Milk and apples are selected for analysis and matrix interference is mitigated by pre-test dilution. As shown in Table S2, the recoveries of the two spiked samples range from 95.28 % to 108.20 %, with RSD values less than 3.92 % (circular dichroism) and 1.58 % (ratiometric FL). These acceptable results signify that the circular dichroism and ratiometric FL dual-mode platform possesses good reliability. To enable in situ rapid screening and point-of-care application, we further evaluate the feasibility of the smartphone-based handheld sensor. Expectedly, the recoveries range from 95.07 % to 105.46 % with RSD values below 3.12 % in handheld detection mode, confirming that the proposed method enables visual and quantitative determination of L-Trp in complex matrices. Meanwhile, UV–visible analysis is utilized as the reference method (Table S3). The relative errors between the proposed method and standard method are found to be below 4.46 %. These findings demonstrate that the proposed method can be applied to quantify L-Trp with high accuracy.

Moreover, to evaluate the robustness of the developed method, recovery experiments are conducted in fetal bovine serum. The results demonstrate that the recoveries range from 97.20 % to 102.52 % with RSD values below 2.09 % (Table S3), indicating that the proposed method exhibits excellent robustness. It is noteworthy that RSD values for the handheld sensor are relatively higher compared to the standard method. The reason may be attributed to inherent limitations in the smartphone-assisted handheld platform, such as non-uniform light source distribution and background FL interference from the 96-well plates. This is a critical issue that must be addressed for smartphone-based handheld sensors. In the future, we will focus on optimizing the light source design and software algorithm. Addressing this issue will significantly advance the development of intelligent handheld technologies, facilitating their widespread adoption and real-world implementation.

As summarized in Table S4, the designed L-CDs-based sensor can effectively differentiate the enantiomers of Trp by triple modes and has a broader linear range. More importantly, with the support of a smartphone and 3D-printing handheld device, the laboratory-based sensing method enables quantitative, easy-handling, and instrument-free in situ detection of L-Trp with satisfactory sensitivity. Compared with reported methods for L-Trp detection, the proposed multifunctional sensing platform primarily addresses the aforementioned challenges and exhibits the following advantages: (1) In terms of chiral probe construction, the method adopts L-CDs with good environmental compatibility, low toxicity, and low cost, without the need of metal ions participation, which simplifies both synthesis and testing procedures. (2) The chiral system based on ratiometric FL sensing significantly enhances color contrast and improves detection sensitivity due to its self-calibration function. (3) Compared with single-mode assays, integrating ratiometric FL, circular dichroism, and handheld imaging triple-modes provides flexible options to satisfy different testing requirements, thereby greatly improving detection accuracy and reliability. (4) The user-friendly handheld platform enables on-site testing without bulky equipment while ensuring accuracy and reliability, immensely advancing its practical applications and paving a new way for enantioselectivity testing.

In summary, a novel chiral L-CDs with excellent luminescence properties is rationally designed by a one-step hydrothermal method without chiral modification. Benefitting from the hydrogen bonding interactions between L-CDs and L-Trp, L-CDs exhibit outstanding ratiometric FL and circular dichroism recognition capabilities for Trp enantiomers. Combined with a smartphone with a self-programming applet, a portable handheld sensor integrated with L-CDs, 96-well microplates, and a 3D-printing device is constructed for rapid, low-cost, instrument-free, and visual quantitative analysis of L-Trp in real-time/on-site conditions. The portable and direct read-out handheld sensor shows good stability, acceptable reproducibility, and high selectivity, which can be further applied for detection of L-Trp in real samples. This work presents a straightforward route to construct multi-mode chiral sensors for enantioselective differentiation, which is believed to have great potential in chiral recognition, biomedical diagnosis, and drug screening.

Dan Chang: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Shuo Wang: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Yanan Yan: Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Jianyang Feng: Validation, Investigation, Data curation. Jianan Pei: Validation, Investigation, Data curation. Hu Shi: Validation, Investigation, Data curation. Yongxing Yang: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Lihong Shi: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Shaomin Shuang: Project administration, Funding acquisition. Chuan Dong: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the Youth Project of Shanxi Basic Research Program (202403021222007), Fundamental Research Program of Shanxi Province (202303021221079), and National Natural Science Foundation of China (22274090).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2025.162592.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
